title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when  generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens

input_row.title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when  generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens
input_row.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
input_row.rationale: Stored access policies provide centralized control over SAS token access, allowing administrators to update permissions or revoke access. This approach strengthens security by reducing the risk of unauthorized access to storage resources.
input_row.audit: It is not currently possible to retrieve a list of all generated SAS tokens to check if they were associated with a SAP during creation. An SAS token that has been created with a SAP will contain an si parameter that references the stored access policy identifier associated with the SAS, e.g. si=. The si parameter will be absent from an SAS token created without a SAP.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal To create a SAP for a queue: 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Data storage, click Queues. 4. Click the name of a queue. 5. Under Settings, click Access policy. 6. Under Stored access policies, click + Add policy. 7. Enter an Identifier. 8. From the Permissions drop-down, select appropriate permissions for the policy. 9. Set an appropriate Start time for the policy. 10. Set an appropriate Expiry time for the policy. 11. Click OK. 12. Click Save. 13. Repeat steps 1-12 as needed to create SAP. When generating SAS, select a SAP from the Stored access policy drop-down. If SAS have been created without a SAP, the SAS can be revoked by regenerating the storage account access keys: Note: Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Security + networking, click Access keys. 4. Next to each key, click Rotate key. 5. Click Yes to confirm. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 as needed to revoke SAS.
input_row.impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.default_value: By default, stored access policies are not associated with SAS. To use a stored access policy, it must be explicitly created and linked to the SAS at the time of creation.
input_row.additional_information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens, from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_s3_bucket_shared_access_signature_sap_association_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would perform the following checks:

1. **List All S3 Buckets:** 
   - Use AWS CLI command: `aws s3api list-buckets`
   - This will gather all S3 buckets to examine shared access signature (SAS) configurations.

2. **Check Bucket Policies and Permissions:**
   - For each S3 bucket, retrieve policies associated with it using:
     - Boto3 method: `s3.get_bucket_policy(Bucket='bucket_name')`
     - AWS CLI command: `aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket bucket_name`
   - Validate that access permissions allow for the implementation of stored access policies.

3. **Check for Shared Access Signature (SAS) tokens:**
   - AWS does not have a direct analogue to Azure's Shared Access Signature; however, we can check for public access and evaluate the bucket policy.
   - Use AWS CLI commands to check public access:
     - `aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket bucket_name`
   - Additionally, confirm bucket cross-account access configurations to check the extent to which the bucket is exposed:
     - `aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket bucket_name`
  
4. **Ensure Best Practices:**
   - Refer to AWS best practices for secure access management which may indirectly help in assessing if stored access style policies are practiced effectively.

**Pass/Fail Criteria:**
- The check will pass if:
  - No SAS equivalent permissions exposed that overwrite security policies (i.e., check for public access settings, permissions).
  - Cross-account access is only for specified necessary entities.
  
- It will fail if:
  - The bucket is publicly accessible.
  - Permissions allow broad access not defined in stored access-like policies. 

This setup will ensure compliance with principles akin to using stored access policies in Azure, adapted to AWS practices.
